Individual countries, regions, and major cities are served by [particular churches](/wiki/Particular_church "Particular church") known as [dioceses](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") in the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church"), or [eparchies](/wiki/Eparchies "Eparchies") in the [Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches"), each of which are overseen by a bishop. As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Catholic_Church&action=edit), the Catholic Church [has 3,171 dioceses](/wiki/List_of_Catholic_dioceses_(structured_view) "List of Catholic dioceses (structured view)") globally.[[198]](#cite_note-sees-206) The bishops in a particular country are members of a national or regional episcopal conference.[[199]](#cite_note-207)

Dioceses are divided into parishes, each with one or more [priests](/wiki/Priesthood_(Catholic_Church) "Priesthood (Catholic Church)"), [deacons](/wiki/Deacon "Deacon"), or [lay ecclesial ministers](/wiki/Lay_ecclesial_ministers "Lay ecclesial ministers").[[200]](#cite_note-OneFaith52-208) Parishes are responsible for the day to day celebration of the sacraments and pastoral care of the laity.[[201]](#cite_note-209) As of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Catholic_Church&action=edit) there are approximately 221,700 parishes worldwide.[[7]](#cite_note-Vermont_winter-7)

In the Latin Church, Catholic men may serve as deacons or priests by receiving sacramental [ordination](/wiki/Holy_Orders "Holy Orders"). Men and women may serve as [extraordinary ministers of Holy Communion](/wiki/Extraordinary_ministers_of_Holy_Communion "Extraordinary ministers of Holy Communion"), as readers ([lectors](/wiki/Reader_(liturgy) "Reader (liturgy)")), or as [altar servers](/wiki/Altar_servers "Altar servers"). Historically, boys and men have only been permitted to serve as altar servers; however, since the 1990s, girls and women have also been permitted.[[202]](#cite_note-Apostalicae86-210)[[note 9]](#cite_note-211)

Catholics may enter into [consecrated life](/wiki/Consecrated_life "Consecrated life") either on an individual basis, as a [hermit](/wiki/Hermit "Hermit") or [consecrated virgin](/wiki/Consecrated_virgin "Consecrated virgin"), or by joining an [institute of consecrated life](/wiki/Institute_of_consecrated_life "Institute of consecrated life") (a religious institute or a [secular institute](/wiki/Secular_institute "Secular institute")) in which to take [vows](/wiki/Religious_vows "Religious vows") confirming their desire to follow the three [evangelical counsels](/wiki/Evangelical_counsels "Evangelical counsels") of [chastity](/wiki/Chastity "Chastity"), poverty and obedience.[[203]](#cite_note-Canons573-746-212) Examples of institutes of consecrated life are the [Benedictines](/wiki/Benedictines "Benedictines"), the [Carmelites](/wiki/Carmelites "Carmelites"), the [Dominicans](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order"), the [Franciscans](/wiki/Franciscan "Franciscan"), the [Missionaries of Charity](/wiki/Missionaries_of_Charity "Missionaries of Charity"), the [Legionaries of Christ](/wiki/Legionaries_of_Christ "Legionaries of Christ") and the [Sisters of Mercy](/wiki/Sisters_of_Mercy "Sisters of Mercy").[[203]](#cite_note-Canons573-746-212)

"Religious institutes" is a modern term encompassing both "[religious orders](/wiki/Religious_order_(Catholic) "Religious order (Catholic)")" and "[religious congregations](/wiki/Religious_congregations "Religious congregations")", which were once distinguished in [canon law](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law").[[204]](#cite_note-213) The terms "religious order" and "religious institute" tend to be used as synonyms colloquially.[[205]](#cite_note-214)

By means of [Catholic charities](/wiki/Catholic_charities "Catholic charities") and beyond, the Catholic Church is the largest non-government provider of [education](/wiki/Catholic_school "Catholic school") and [health care](/wiki/Catholic_Church_and_health_care "Catholic Church and health care") in the world.[[206]](#cite_note-Geopolitics-215)

As of 2020 Catholicism is the second-largest [religious body](/wiki/Religious_denomination "Religious denomination") in the world after [Sunni Islam](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam").[[207]](#cite_note-216) Catholics represent about half of all Christians.[[208]](#cite_note-217) According to the *[World Christian Database](/wiki/World_Christian_Database "World Christian Database")*, there are 1.279 billion Catholics constituting 47.8% of 2.674 billion Christians globally, as of 2026.[[4]](#cite_note-WorldChristianDatabase-4) According to the *[Annuario Pontificio](/wiki/Annuario_Pontificio "Annuario Pontificio")*, church membership, defined as baptized Catholics, was 1.406 billion at the end of 2023, which was 17.4% of the world population:[[5]](#cite_note-Yearbook_3/20/2025-5) Under Pope Francis the church membership grew by almost 11%, with growth concentrated in Africa and loss in Europe.[[209]](#cite_note-218)

Brazil has the largest Catholic population in the world, followed by Mexico, the Philippines and the United States.[[210]](#cite_note-219)

Geographic distribution of Catholics worldwide continues to shift, with 20.0% in [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), 47.8% in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), 11.0% in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), 20.4% in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and 0.8% in [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania").[[5]](#cite_note-Yearbook_3/20/2025-5)

Catholic ministers include ordained clergy, [lay ecclesial ministers](/wiki/Lay_ecclesial_ministers "Lay ecclesial ministers"), [missionaries](/wiki/Missionaries "Missionaries") and [catechists](/wiki/Catechists "Catechists"). Also as of the end of 2023, there were 463,859 ordained clergy, including 5,430 bishops, 406,996 priests (diocesan and religious) and 51,433 deacons (permanent).[[5]](#cite_note-Yearbook_3/20/2025-5) Non-ordained ministers, as at October 2024, include 2,883,049 catechists and 413,561 lay missionaries.[[211]](#cite_note-220)

Catholics who have committed to religious or consecrated life as a state of life or relational vocation include 49,414 male religious (as of 2022) and 589,423 women religious (as of 2023). These are not ordained, nor generally called "ministers" unless also engaged in one of the lay minister categories above.[[5]](#cite_note-Yearbook_3/20/2025-5)

## Doctrine

Main article: [Catholic theology](/wiki/Catholic_theology "Catholic theology")

Catholic doctrine has developed over the centuries, reflecting direct teachings of early Christians, formal definitions of [heretical](/wiki/Heresy_in_Christianity "Heresy in Christianity") and [orthodox](/wiki/Orthodoxy "Orthodoxy") beliefs by [ecumenical councils](/wiki/Ecumenical_councils "Ecumenical councils") and in [papal bulls](/wiki/Papal_bull "Papal bull"), and theological debate by scholars. The church believes that it is continually guided by the Holy Spirit as it discerns new theological issues and is protected [infallibly](/wiki/Infallibility_of_the_Church "Infallibility of the Church") from falling into doctrinal error when a firm decision on an issue is reached.[[212]](#cite_note-221)[[213]](#cite_note-222)

It teaches that revelation has one common source, [God](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity"), and two distinct modes of transmission: Sacred Scripture and [Sacred Tradition](/wiki/Sacred_Tradition "Sacred Tradition"),[[214]](#cite_note-223)[[215]](#cite_note-LumenG3-224) and that these are authentically interpreted by the [Magisterium](/wiki/Magisterium "Magisterium").[[216]](#cite_note-225)[[217]](#cite_note-226) Sacred Scripture consists of the 73 books of the [Catholic Bible](/wiki/Catholic_Bible "Catholic Bible"), consisting of 46 [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") and 27 [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") writings. Sacred Tradition consists of those teachings believed by the church to have been handed down since the time of the Apostles.[[218]](#cite_note-Schreck16-227) Sacred Scripture and Sacred Tradition are collectively known as the "deposit of faith" (*depositum fidei* in Latin). These are in turn interpreted by the Magisterium (from *magister*, Latin for "teacher"), the church's teaching authority, which is exercised by the pope and the [College of Bishops](/wiki/College_of_Bishops "College of Bishops") in union with the pope.[[219]](#cite_note-Schreck30-228) Catholic doctrine is authoritatively summarized in the *[Catechism of the Catholic Church](/wiki/Catechism_of_the_Catholic_Church "Catechism of the Catholic Church")*, published by the Holy See.[[220]](#cite_note-cat-229)[[221]](#cite_note-230)

### Nature of God

Main article: [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Shield-Trinity-Scutum-Fidei-English.svg/250px-Shield-Trinity-Scutum-Fidei-English.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Shield-Trinity-Scutum-Fidei-English.svg)

This diagram depicts the [Shield of the Trinity](/wiki/Shield_of_the_Trinity "Shield of the Trinity"), which expresses many aspects of the doctrine of the Trinity.

The Catholic Church holds that there is one [eternal](/wiki/Attributes_of_God_in_Christianity#Eternity "Attributes of God in Christianity") God, who exists as a *[perichoresis](/wiki/Perichoresis "Perichoresis")* ("mutual indwelling") of three *[hypostases](/wiki/Hypostasis_(philosophy_and_religion) "Hypostasis (philosophy and religion)")*, or "persons": [God the Father](/wiki/God_the_Father#Christianity "God the Father"); [God the Son](/wiki/God_the_Son "God the Son"); and [God the Holy Spirit](/wiki/God_the_Holy_Spirit "God the Holy Spirit") (also called the Holy Ghost), which together are called the "Holy Trinity".[[222]](#cite_note-232_252-231)

Catholics believe that Jesus Christ is the "Second Person" of the Trinity, God the Son. In an event known as the [Incarnation](/wiki/Incarnation_(Christianity) "Incarnation (Christianity)"), through the power of the Holy Spirit, God became united with human nature through the conception of Christ in the womb of the [Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Veneration_of_Mary_in_the_Catholic_Church "Veneration of Mary in the Catholic Church"). Christ, therefore, is understood as being both fully divine and fully human, including possessing a human [soul](/wiki/Soul "Soul"). It is taught that Christ's mission on earth included giving people his teachings and providing his example for them to follow as recorded in the four [Gospels](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel").[[223]](#cite_note-McGrath-232) Jesus is believed to have remained sinless while on earth, and to have allowed himself to be unjustly executed by [crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus"), as a sacrifice of himself to reconcile humanity to God; this reconciliation is known as the [Paschal Mystery](/wiki/Paschal_Mystery "Paschal Mystery").[[224]](#cite_note-233) The Greek term "Christ" and the Hebrew "Messiah" both mean "anointed one", referring to the Christian belief that Jesus' death and resurrection are the fulfilment of the Old Testament's [messianic prophecies](/wiki/Jesus_and_messianic_prophecy "Jesus and messianic prophecy").[[225]](#cite_note-Kreeft71-234)

The Catholic Church teaches dogmatically that "the Holy Spirit proceeds eternally from the Father and the Son, not as from two principles but as from one single principle".[[226]](#cite_note-235) It holds that the Father, as the "principle without principle", is the first origin of the Spirit, but also that he, as Father of the only Son, is with the Son the single principle from which the Spirit proceeds.[[227]](#cite_note-236) This belief is expressed in the *[Filioque](/wiki/Filioque "Filioque")* clause which was added to the Latin version of the Nicene Creed of 381 but not included in the Greek versions of the creed used in Eastern Christianity.[[228]](#cite_note-245_248-237)

### Nature of the church

Main article: [Catholic ecclesiology](/wiki/Catholic_ecclesiology "Catholic ecclesiology")

The Catholic Church teaches that it is the "[one true church](/wiki/One_true_church "One true church")",[[8]](#cite_note-Catholic_News_Service-9)[[229]](#cite_note-238) "the universal sacrament of salvation for the human race",[[230]](#cite_note-GAUDIUM-239)[[231]](#cite_note-ReferenceC-240) and "the one true religion".[[232]](#cite_note-241) According to the *Catechism*, the Catholic Church is further described in the Nicene Creed as the "one, holy, catholic, and apostolic Church".[[233]](#cite_note-242) These are collectively known as the [Four Marks of the Church](/wiki/Four_Marks_of_the_Church "Four Marks of the Church"). The church teaches that its founder is Jesus Christ.[[234]](#cite_note-Kreeft98-243)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBokenkotter200430-38) The [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") records several events considered integral to the establishment of the Catholic Church, including Jesus' activities and teaching and his appointment of the [apostles](/wiki/Apostles_in_the_New_Testament "Apostles in the New Testament") as witnesses to his ministry, suffering, and resurrection. The [Great Commission](/wiki/Great_Commission "Great Commission"), after his resurrection, instructed the apostles to continue his work. The coming of the Holy Spirit upon the apostles, in an event known as [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost"), is seen as the beginning of the public ministry of the Catholic Church.[[36]](#cite_note-Barry48-41) The church teaches that all duly consecrated bishops have a lineal succession from the apostles of Christ, known as [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession").[[235]](#cite_note-OneFaith46-244) In particular, the Bishop of Rome (the pope) is considered the successor to the apostle [Simon Peter](/wiki/Simon_Peter "Simon Peter"), a position from which he derives his [supremacy](/wiki/Papal_supremacy "Papal supremacy") over the church.[[236]](#cite_note-245)

Catholic belief holds that the church "is the continuing presence of Jesus on earth"[[237]](#cite_note-Schreck131-246) and that it alone possesses the full means of [salvation](/wiki/Salvation_(Christianity) "Salvation (Christianity)").[[238]](#cite_note-CCC_816-247) Through the [passion](/wiki/Passion_(Christianity) "Passion (Christianity)") (suffering) of Christ leading to his [crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus") as described in the Gospels, it is said Christ made himself an oblation to God the Father to [reconcile](/wiki/Atonement_in_Christianity "Atonement in Christianity") humanity to God;[[239]](#cite_note-608_-248) the [Resurrection of Jesus](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus") makes him the firstborn from the dead, the first among many brethren.[[240]](#cite_note-249) By reconciling with God and following Christ's words and deeds, an individual can enter the [Kingdom of God](/wiki/Kingdom_of_God_(Christianity) "Kingdom of God (Christianity)").[[241]](#cite_note-OneFaith26-250) The church sees its liturgy and sacraments as perpetuating the graces achieved through Christ's sacrifice to strengthen a person's relationship with Christ and aid in overcoming sin.[[242]](#cite_note-CoCCC_Paschal-251)

### Final judgement

Main article: [Last Judgment § Catholicism](/wiki/Last_Judgment#Catholicism "Last Judgment")

The Catholic Church teaches that, immediately after death, the [soul](/wiki/Soul "Soul") of each person will receive a [particular judgement](/wiki/Particular_judgement "Particular judgement") from God, based on their [sins](/wiki/Sin "Sin") and their relationship to Christ.[[243]](#cite_note-252)[[244]](#cite_note-Schreck397-253) This teaching also attests to another day when Christ will sit in universal judgement of all mankind. This [final judgement](/wiki/Final_judgement "Final judgement"), according to the Church's teaching, will bring an end to human history and mark the beginning of both a new and better heaven and earth ruled by God in righteousness.[[245]](#cite_note-254)

Depending on the judgement rendered following death, it is believed that a soul may enter one of three states of the afterlife:

* [Heaven](/wiki/Heaven_in_Christianity#Roman_Catholicism "Heaven in Christianity") is a state of unending union with the divine nature of God, not ontologically, but by grace. It is an eternal life, in which the soul contemplates God in ceaseless [beatitude](/wiki/Beatific_vision "Beatific vision").[[246]](#cite_note-255)
* [Purgatory](/wiki/Purgatory "Purgatory") is a temporary condition for the purification of souls who, although destined for Heaven, are not fully detached from sin and thus cannot enter Heaven immediately.[[247]](#cite_note-256) In Purgatory, the soul suffers, and is purged and perfected. Souls in purgatory may be aided in reaching heaven by the prayers of the faithful on earth and by the [intercession of saints](/wiki/Intercession_of_saints "Intercession of saints").[[248]](#cite_note-257)
* [Final Damnation](/wiki/Hell_in_Christian_beliefs#Roman_Catholicism "Hell in Christian beliefs"): Finally, those who persist in living in a state of mortal sin and do not repent before death subject themselves to hell, an everlasting separation from God.[[249]](#cite_note-258) The church teaches that no one is condemned to hell without having freely decided to reject God.[[250]](#cite_note-259) No one is [predestined](/wiki/Predestined "Predestined") to hell and no one can determine with absolute certainty who has been condemned to hell.[[251]](#cite_note-260) Catholicism teaches that through God's mercy a person can repent at any point before death, be illuminated with the truth of the Catholic faith, and thus obtain salvation.[[252]](#cite_note-Luke23-261) Some Catholic theologians have speculated that the souls of unbaptized infants and non-Christians without mortal sin but who die in [original sin](/wiki/Original_sin "Original sin") are assigned to [limbo](/wiki/Limbo "Limbo"), although this is not an official [dogma](/wiki/Dogma_in_the_Catholic_Church "Dogma in the Catholic Church") of the church.[[253]](#cite_note-262)

While the Catholic Church teaches that it alone possesses the full means of salvation,[[238]](#cite_note-CCC_816-247) it also acknowledges that the Holy Spirit can make use of [Christian communities](/wiki/Ecclesial_Community "Ecclesial Community") separated from itself to "impel towards Catholic unity"[[254]](#cite_note-ewtn.com-263) and "tend and lead toward the Catholic Church",[[254]](#cite_note-ewtn.com-263) and thus bring people to salvation, because these separated communities contain some elements of proper doctrine, albeit admixed with [errors](/wiki/Heresy "Heresy"). It teaches that anyone who is saved is saved through the Catholic Church but that people can be saved outside of the ordinary means known as [baptism of desire](/wiki/Baptism_of_desire "Baptism of desire"), and by pre-baptismal martyrdom, known as [baptism of blood](/wiki/Baptism_of_blood "Baptism of blood"), as well as when conditions of [invincible ignorance](/wiki/Invincible_ignorance "Invincible ignorance") are present, although invincible ignorance in itself is not a means of salvation.[[255]](#cite_note-264) The [Vatican II](/wiki/Vatican_II "Vatican II") document [*Lumen* *gentium*](/wiki/Lumen_gentium "Lumen gentium") further clarifies the possibility of salvation of those who "through no fault of their own, do not know the Gospel of Christ or his Church, but who nevertheless seek God with a sincere heart," being "moved by (divine) grace".["*CCC*, 847"](https://www.vatican.va/archive/ENG0015/__P29.HTM). Vatican.va.

### Saints and devotions

Main articles: [Saint](/wiki/Saint "Saint"), [Canonization](/wiki/Canonization "Canonization"), [Veneration](/wiki/Veneration "Veneration"), and [Catholic devotions](/wiki/Catholic_devotions "Catholic devotions")

A saint (also historically known as a hallow) is a person who is recognized as having an exceptional degree of holiness or likeness or closeness to God, while canonization is the act by which a Christian church declares that a person who has died was a saint, upon which declaration the person is included in the "canon", or list, of recognized saints.[[256]](#cite_note-WilsonFischer2005-265)[[257]](#cite_note-DiehlDonnelly2001-266) The first persons honoured as saints were the [martyrs](/wiki/Christian_martyr "Christian martyr"). By the fourth century, however, "[confessors](/wiki/Confessor_of_the_Faith "Confessor of the Faith")"—people who had confessed their faith not by dying but by suffering—began to be venerated publicly.[[258]](#cite_note-ce_beccari-267)

In the Catholic Church, both in Latin and Eastern Catholic churches, the act of canonization is reserved to the [Apostolic See](/wiki/Apostolic_See "Apostolic See") and occurs at the conclusion of a long process requiring extensive proof that the candidate for canonization lived and died in such an exemplary and holy way that he is worthy to be recognized as a saint. The church's official recognition of sanctity implies that the person is now in [Heaven](/wiki/Heaven "Heaven") and that he may be publicly invoked and mentioned officially in the [liturgy](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") of the church, including in the [Litany of the Saints](/wiki/Litany_of_the_Saints "Litany of the Saints"). [Canonization](/wiki/Canonization "Canonization") allows universal veneration of the saint in the liturgy of the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite"); for permission to venerate merely locally, only [beatification](/wiki/Beatification "Beatification") is needed.[[259]](#cite_note-268)

[Devotions](/wiki/Catholic_devotions "Catholic devotions") are "external practices of piety" which are not part of the official liturgy of the Catholic Church but are part of the popular spiritual practices of Catholics.[[260]](#cite_note-269) These include various practices regarding the veneration of the saints, especially [veneration of the Virgin Mary](/wiki/Veneration_of_Mary_in_the_Catholic_Church "Veneration of Mary in the Catholic Church"). Other devotional practices include the [Stations of the Cross](/wiki/Stations_of_the_Cross "Stations of the Cross"), the [Sacred Heart](/wiki/Sacred_Heart "Sacred Heart") of Jesus, the [Holy Face of Jesus](/wiki/Holy_Face_of_Jesus "Holy Face of Jesus"),[[261]](#cite_note-etwndevoti-270) the various [scapulars](/wiki/Scapular "Scapular"), novenas to various saints,[[262]](#cite_note-popdevos-271) [pilgrimages](/wiki/Christian_pilgrimage "Christian pilgrimage")[[263]](#cite_note-272) and devotions to the [Blessed Sacrament](/wiki/Eucharist_(Catholic_Church) "Eucharist (Catholic Church)"),[[262]](#cite_note-popdevos-271) and the veneration of [saintly images](/wiki/Santo_(art) "Santo (art)") such as the *[santos](/wiki/Santo_(art) "Santo (art)")*.[[264]](#cite_note-273) The bishops at the Second Vatican Council reminded Catholics that "devotions should be so drawn up that they harmonize with the liturgical seasons, accord with the sacred liturgy, are in some fashion derived from it, and lead the people to it, since, in fact, the liturgy by its very nature far surpasses any of them."[[265]](#cite_note-274)

Main articles: [Veneration of Mary in the Catholic Church](/wiki/Veneration_of_Mary_in_the_Catholic_Church "Veneration of Mary in the Catholic Church"), [Mariology of the Catholic Church](/wiki/Mariology_of_the_Catholic_Church "Mariology of the Catholic Church"), [Mariology of the saints](/wiki/Mariology_of_the_saints "Mariology of the saints"), and [Mariology of the popes](/wiki/Mariology_of_the_popes "Mariology of the popes")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Antolinez-inmaculada-bilbao.jpg/250px-Antolinez-inmaculada-bilbao.jpg)](/wiki/File:Antolinez-inmaculada-bilbao.jpg)

The [Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Veneration_of_Mary_in_the_Catholic_Church "Veneration of Mary in the Catholic Church") is highly regarded in the Catholic Church, proclaiming her as [Mother of God](/wiki/Mother_of_God "Mother of God"), [free from original sin](/wiki/Immaculate_Conception "Immaculate Conception") and an [intercessor](/wiki/Intercessor "Intercessor").

[Catholic Mariology](/wiki/Catholic_Mariology "Catholic Mariology") deals with the [dogmas](/wiki/Dogma_in_the_Catholic_Church "Dogma in the Catholic Church") and teachings concerning the life of [Mary, mother of Jesus](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus"), as well as the veneration of Mary by the faithful. Mary is held in special regard, declared the [Mother of God](/wiki/Mother_of_God "Mother of God") ([Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): Θεοτόκος, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Greek "Romanization of Greek"): *Theotokos*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'God-bearer'), and believed as dogma to have remained a [virgin throughout her life](/wiki/Perpetual_virginity_of_Mary "Perpetual virginity of Mary").[[266]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-275) Further teachings include the doctrines of the [Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Immaculate_Conception "Immaculate Conception") (her own conception without the stain of original sin) and the [Assumption of Mary](/wiki/Assumption_of_Mary "Assumption of Mary") (that her body was assumed directly into heaven at the end of her life). Both of these doctrines were defined as infallible dogma, by [Pope Pius IX](/wiki/Pope_Pius_IX "Pope Pius IX") in 1854 and [Pope Pius XII](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XII "Pope Pius XII") in 1950 respectively,[[267]](#cite_note-Barry,_p._106-276) but only after consulting with the Catholic bishops throughout the world to ascertain that this is a Catholic belief.[[268]](#cite_note-277) In the Eastern Catholic churches, however, they continue to celebrate the feast of the Assumption under the name of the [Dormition of the Mother of God](/wiki/Dormition_of_the_Mother_of_God "Dormition of the Mother of God") on the same date.[[269]](#cite_note-278) The teaching that Mary died before being assumed significantly precedes the idea that she did not. St John Damascene wrote that "St Juvenal, Bishop of Jerusalem, at the Council of Chalcedon (451), made known to the Emperor Marcian and Pulcheria, who wished to possess the body of the Mother of God, that Mary died in the presence of all the Apostles, but that her tomb, when opened, upon the request of St Thomas, was found empty; wherefrom the Apostles concluded that the body was taken up to Heaven."[[270]](#cite_note-279)

[Devotions to Mary](/wiki/Marian_devotions "Marian devotions") are part of Catholic piety but are distinct from the worship of God.[[271]](#cite_note-280) Practices include prayers and [Marian art](/wiki/Marian_art "Marian art"), [music](/wiki/Catholic_Marian_music "Catholic Marian music") and [architecture](/wiki/Catholic_Marian_church_buildings "Catholic Marian church buildings"). Several [liturgical Marian feasts](/wiki/Marian_feast_days "Marian feast days") are celebrated throughout the [Church Year](/wiki/Church_Year "Church Year") and she is honoured with [many titles](/wiki/Titles_of_Mary "Titles of Mary") such as [Queen of Heaven](/wiki/Queen_of_Heaven "Queen of Heaven"). [Pope Paul VI](/wiki/Pope_Paul_VI "Pope Paul VI") called her [Mother of the Church](/wiki/Mother_of_the_Church "Mother of the Church") because, by giving birth to Christ, she is considered to be the spiritual mother to each member of the [Body of Christ](/wiki/Body_of_Christ "Body of Christ").[[267]](#cite_note-Barry,_p._106-276) Because of her influential role in the life of Jesus, prayers and devotions such as the [Hail Mary](/wiki/Hail_Mary "Hail Mary"), the [Rosary](/wiki/Rosary "Rosary"), the [Salve Regina](/wiki/Salve_Regina "Salve Regina") and the [Memorare](/wiki/Memorare "Memorare") are common Catholic practices.[[272]](#cite_note-281) [Pilgrimage](/wiki/Christian_pilgrimage "Christian pilgrimage") to the sites of several [Marian apparitions](/wiki/Marian_apparitions "Marian apparitions") affirmed by the church, such as [Lourdes](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Lourdes "Our Lady of Lourdes"), [Fátima](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_F%C3%A1tima "Our Lady of Fátima") and [Guadalupe](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Guadalupe "Our Lady of Guadalupe"),[[273]](#cite_note-282) are also popular Catholic devotions.[[274]](#cite_note-283)

## Sacraments

Main article: [Sacraments of the Catholic Church](/wiki/Sacraments_of_the_Catholic_Church "Sacraments of the Catholic Church")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/20190529_Spain_and_Portugal_El_Camino_Pilgrimage_1063_%2848002601588%29.jpg/250px-20190529_Spain_and_Portugal_El_Camino_Pilgrimage_1063_%2848002601588%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:20190529_Spain_and_Portugal_El_Camino_Pilgrimage_1063_(48002601588).jpg)

[Holy Mass](/wiki/Mass_in_the_Catholic_Church "Mass in the Catholic Church") at the [Sanctuary of Our Lady of Fátima](/wiki/Sanctuary_of_Our_Lady_of_F%C3%A1tima "Sanctuary of Our Lady of Fátima"), Portugal. The [host](/wiki/Sacramental_bread "Sacramental bread") and the [chalice](/wiki/Chalice "Chalice") are displayed to the people immediately after the [consecration](/wiki/Consecration_in_Christianity "Consecration in Christianity") of the bread and wine into the [Holy Body and Blood of Christ](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist").

The Catholic Church teaches that it was entrusted with seven sacraments that were instituted by Christ. The number and nature of the sacraments were defined by several [ecumenical councils](/wiki/Ecumenical_council "Ecumenical council"), most recently the Council of Trent.[[275]](#cite_note-1113_14-284)[[note 10]](#cite_note-285) These are [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism"), [confirmation](/wiki/Confirmation "Confirmation"), the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist"), [penance](/wiki/Sacrament_of_Penance_(Catholic_Church) "Sacrament of Penance (Catholic Church)"), [anointing of the sick](/wiki/Anointing_of_the_Sick_(Catholic_Church) "Anointing of the Sick (Catholic Church)") (formerly called Extreme Unction, one of the "[Last Rites](/wiki/Last_Rites "Last Rites")"), [holy orders](/wiki/Holy_orders "Holy orders") and [holy matrimony](/wiki/Catholic_marriage "Catholic marriage"). Sacraments are visible rituals that Catholics see as signs of God's presence and effective channels of God's [grace](/wiki/Grace_(Christianity) "Grace (Christianity)") to all those who receive them with the proper disposition (*[ex opere operato](/wiki/Ex_opere_operato "Ex opere operato")*).[[276]](#cite_note-286) The *[Catechism of the Catholic Church](/wiki/Catechism_of_the_Catholic_Church "Catechism of the Catholic Church")* categorizes the sacraments into three groups, the "sacraments of Christian initiation", "sacraments of healing" and "sacraments at the service of communion and the mission of the faithful". These groups broadly reflect the stages of people's natural and spiritual lives which each sacrament is intended to serve.[[277]](#cite_note-287)

The liturgies of the sacraments are central to the church's mission. According to the *Catechism*:

> In the liturgy of the New Covenant every liturgical action, especially the celebration of the Eucharist and the sacraments, is an encounter between Christ and the Church. The liturgical assembly derives its unity from the "communion of the Holy Spirit" who gathers the children of God into the one Body of Christ. This assembly transcends racial, cultural, social—indeed, all human affinities.[[278]](#cite_note-288)

According to church doctrine, the sacraments of the church require the proper form, matter, and intent to be validly celebrated.[[279]](#cite_note-289) In addition, the [Canon Laws](/wiki/Canon_Law "Canon Law") for both the Latin Church and the Eastern Catholic Churches govern who may licitly celebrate certain sacraments, as well as strict rules about who may receive the sacraments.[[280]](#cite_note-CoCC291-290) Notably, because the church teaches that Christ is [present](/wiki/Real_presence#Catholic_and_Orthodox "Real presence") in the Eucharist,[[281]](#cite_note-Kreeft326-291) those who are conscious of being in a state of mortal sin are forbidden to receive the sacrament until they have received absolution through the [sacrament of Reconciliation](/wiki/Sacrament_of_Reconciliation "Sacrament of Reconciliation") (Penance).[[282]](#cite_note-Kreeft331-292) Catholics are normally obliged to abstain from eating for at least an hour before receiving the sacrament.[[282]](#cite_note-Kreeft331-292) Non-Catholics are ordinarily prohibited from receiving the Eucharist as well.[[280]](#cite_note-CoCC291-290)[[283]](#cite_note-293)

Catholics, even if they were in danger of death and unable to approach a Catholic minister, may not ask for the sacraments of the Eucharist, penance or anointing of the sick from someone, such as a Protestant minister, who is not known to be validly ordained in line with Catholic teaching on ordination.[[284]](#cite_note-294)[[285]](#cite_note-295) Likewise, even in grave and pressing need, Catholic ministers may not administer these sacraments to those who do not manifest Catholic faith in the sacrament. In relation to the churches of Eastern Christianity not in communion with the Holy See, the Catholic Church is less restrictive, declaring that "a certain *communion in sacris*, and so in the Eucharist, given suitable circumstances and the approval of Church authority, is not merely possible but is encouraged."[[286]](#cite_note-296)

### Sacraments of initiation

Main article: [Sacraments of initiation](/wiki/Sacraments_of_initiation "Sacraments of initiation")

#### Baptism

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Bapt%C3%AAme_Cath%C3%A9drale_de_Troyes_290308.jpg/250px-Bapt%C3%AAme_Cath%C3%A9drale_de_Troyes_290308.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bapt%C3%AAme_Cath%C3%A9drale_de_Troyes_290308.jpg)

Baptism of [Augustine of Hippo](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo") as represented in a sculptural group in [Troyes Cathedral](/wiki/Troyes_Cathedral "Troyes Cathedral") (1549), France

As viewed by the Catholic Church, baptism is the first of three sacraments of initiation as a Christian.[[287]](#cite_note-297) It washes away all sins, both [original sin](/wiki/Original_sin "Original sin") and personal actual sins.[[288]](#cite_note-298) It makes a person a member of the church.[[289]](#cite_note-299) As a gratuitous gift of God that requires no merit on the part of the person who is baptized, it is [conferred even on children](/wiki/Infant_baptism "Infant baptism"),[[290]](#cite_note-300) who, though they have no personal sins, need it on account of original sin.[[291]](#cite_note-301)

If a new-born child is in a danger of death, anyone—be it a doctor, a nurse, or a parent—may baptize the child.[[292]](#cite_note-302) Baptism marks a person permanently and cannot be repeated.[[293]](#cite_note-303) The Catholic Church recognizes as valid baptisms conferred even by people who are not Catholics or Christians, provided that they intend to baptize ("to do what the Church does when she baptizes") and that they use the [Trinitarian baptismal formula](/wiki/Trinitarian_formula#Use_in_baptism "Trinitarian formula").[[294]](#cite_note-304)

#### Confirmation

Main article: [Confirmation in the Catholic Church](/wiki/Confirmation_in_the_Catholic_Church "Confirmation in the Catholic Church")

The Catholic Church sees the sacrament of confirmation as required to complete the grace given in baptism.[[295]](#cite_note-305) When adults are baptized, confirmation is normally given immediately afterwards,[[296]](#cite_note-cann883-306) a practice followed even with newly baptized infants in the Eastern Catholic Churches.[[297]](#cite_note-cceo695-307) In the West confirmation of children is delayed until they are old enough to understand or at the bishop's discretion.[[298]](#cite_note-cann891-308) In Western Christianity, particularly Catholicism, the sacrament is called *confirmation*, because it confirms and strengthens the grace of baptism; in the Eastern Churches, it is called *chrismation*, because the essential rite is the anointing of the person with [chrism](/wiki/Chrism "Chrism"),[[299]](#cite_note-Chrism-309) a mixture of [olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil") and some perfumed substance, usually [balsam](/wiki/Oleoresin "Oleoresin"), blessed by a bishop.[[299]](#cite_note-Chrism-309)[[300]](#cite_note-310) Those who receive confirmation must be in a state of grace, which for those who have reached the [age of reason](/wiki/Age_of_reason_(canon_law) "Age of reason (canon law)") means that they should first be cleansed spiritually by the sacrament of Penance; they should also have the intention of receiving the sacrament, and be prepared to show in their lives that they are Christians.[[301]](#cite_note-311)

#### Eucharist

Main article: [Eucharist in the Catholic Church](/wiki/Eucharist_in_the_Catholic_Church "Eucharist in the Catholic Church")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/BentoXVI-51-11052007_%28frag%29.jpg/250px-BentoXVI-51-11052007_%28frag%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:BentoXVI-51-11052007_(frag).jpg)

[Pope Benedict XVI](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XVI "Pope Benedict XVI") celebrates the Eucharist at the [canonization](/wiki/Canonization "Canonization") of [Frei Galvão](/wiki/Frei_Galv%C3%A3o "Frei Galvão") in São Paulo, Brazil on 11 May 2007.

For Catholics, the Eucharist is the sacrament which completes Christian initiation. It is described as "the source and summit of the Christian life".[[302]](#cite_note-312) The ceremony in which a Catholic first receives the Eucharist is known as [First Communion](/wiki/First_Communion "First Communion").[[303]](#cite_note-313)

The Eucharistic celebration, also called the [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") or [Divine liturgy](/wiki/Divine_liturgy "Divine liturgy"), includes prayers and scriptural readings, as well as an offering of bread and wine, which are brought to the [altar](/wiki/Altar "Altar") and [consecrated](/wiki/Consecration#Eucharist "Consecration") by the priest to become the body and the blood of Jesus Christ: the [substance](/wiki/Substance_(philosophy) "Substance (philosophy)") of bread and wine changes despite the [accidents](/wiki/Accident_(philosophy) "Accident (philosophy)") (e.g the visible form) remaining: a change called [transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation").[[304]](#cite_note-314)[[note 11]](#cite_note-315)

The [words of consecration](/wiki/Words_of_consecration "Words of consecration") reflect the words spoken by Jesus during the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper"), where Christ offered his body and blood to his Apostles the night before his crucifixion. The sacrament re-presents (makes present) the sacrifice of Jesus on the cross,[[305]](#cite_note-316) and perpetuates it. Christ's death and resurrection give grace through the sacrament that unites the faithful with Christ and one another, remits venial sin, and aids against committing moral sin (though mortal sin itself is forgiven through the sacrament of penance).[[306]](#cite_note-ccc1392-317)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Convento_de_San_Francisco_-_Ciudad_de_M%C3%A9xico_-_Creyente.jpg/250px-Convento_de_San_Francisco_-_Ciudad_de_M%C3%A9xico_-_Creyente.jpg)](/wiki/File:Convento_de_San_Francisco_-_Ciudad_de_M%C3%A9xico_-_Creyente.jpg)

A Catholic prays in a church in Mexico.

### Sacraments of healing

#### Penance

Main article: [Sacrament of Penance](/wiki/Sacrament_of_Penance "Sacrament of Penance")

The sacrament of penance (also called Reconciliation, Forgiveness, Confession, and Conversion[[307]](#cite_note-318)) exists for the conversion of those who, after baptism, separate themselves from Christ by sin.[[308]](#cite_note-319) Essential to this sacrament are acts both by the sinner (examination of conscience, contrition with a determination not to sin again, confession to a priest, and performance of some act to repair the damage caused by sin) and by the priest (determination of the act of reparation to be performed and [absolution](/wiki/Absolution "Absolution")).[[309]](#cite_note-320)

Serious sins ([mortal sins](/wiki/Mortal_sin "Mortal sin")) should be confessed at least once a year and always before receiving the Eucharist, while confession of [venial sins](/wiki/Venial_sin "Venial sin") also is recommended.[[310]](#cite_note-321) The priest is bound under the severest penalties to maintain the "[seal of confession](/wiki/Seal_of_confession "Seal of confession")", absolute secrecy about any sins revealed to him in confession.[[311]](#cite_note-322)

#### Anointing of the sick

Main article: [Anointing of the Sick in the Catholic Church](/wiki/Anointing_of_the_Sick_in_the_Catholic_Church "Anointing of the Sick in the Catholic Church")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/ae/Extreme_Unction_Rogier_Van_der_Weyden.jpg)](/wiki/File:Extreme_Unction_Rogier_Van_der_Weyden.jpg)

The *[Seven Sacraments Altarpiece](/wiki/Seven_Sacraments_Altarpiece "Seven Sacraments Altarpiece")* [triptych](/wiki/Triptych "Triptych") painting of Extreme Unction (anointing of the sick) with oil being administered by a priest during last rites. [Rogier van der Weyden](/wiki/Rogier_van_der_Weyden "Rogier van der Weyden"), 1445.

While chrism is used only for the three sacraments that cannot be repeated, a different oil is used by a priest or bishop to bless a Catholic who, because of illness or old age, has begun to be in danger of death.[[312]](#cite_note-323) This sacrament, known as anointing of the sick, is believed to give comfort, peace, courage and, if the sick person is unable to make a confession, even forgiveness of sins.[[313]](#cite_note-324)

The sacrament is also referred to as *unction*, and in the past as *extreme unction*, and it is one of the three sacraments that constitute the [last rites](/wiki/Last_rites "Last rites"), together with Penance and [Viaticum](/wiki/Viaticum "Viaticum") (Eucharist).[[314]](#cite_note-325)

### Sacraments at the service of communion

According to the Catechism, there are two sacraments of [communion](/wiki/Koinonia "Koinonia") directed towards the salvation of others: priesthood and marriage.[[315]](#cite_note-326) Within the general vocation to be a Christian, these two sacraments "consecrate to specific mission or [vocation](/wiki/Vocation "Vocation") among the people of God. Men receive the holy orders to feed the church by the word and [grace](/wiki/Grace_(Christianity) "Grace (Christianity)"). Spouses marry so that their love may be fortified to fulfil duties of their state".[[316]](#cite_note-327)

#### Holy orders

Main article: [Holy orders in the Catholic Church](/wiki/Holy_orders_in_the_Catholic_Church "Holy orders in the Catholic Church")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Priesterweihe_in_Schwyz_2.jpg/250px-Priesterweihe_in_Schwyz_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Priesterweihe_in_Schwyz_2.jpg)

Priests lay their hands on the ordinands during the rite of ordination.

The sacrament of holy orders consecrates and deputes some Christians to serve the whole body as members of three degrees or orders: episcopate (bishops), presbyterate (priests) and diaconate (deacons).[[317]](#cite_note-cann10081009-328)[[318]](#cite_note-329) The church has defined rules on who may be ordained into the [clergy](/wiki/Clergy#Catholicism "Clergy"). In the Latin Church the priesthood is generally restricted to celibate men, and the episcopate is always restricted to celibate men.[[319]](#cite_note-330) Men who are already married may be ordained in certain Eastern Catholic churches in most countries,[[320]](#cite_note-331) and the personal ordinariates and may become deacons even in the Latin Church[[321]](#cite_note-CCL1031-332)[[322]](#cite_note-CCL1037-333) (see [clerical marriage](/wiki/Clerical_marriage "Clerical marriage")). After becoming a Catholic priest, a man may not marry (see [clerical celibacy](/wiki/Clerical_celibacy "Clerical celibacy")) unless he is formally [laicized](/wiki/Laicized "Laicized").[[323]](#cite_note-334)

All clergy, whether deacons, priests or bishops, may preach, teach, baptize, witness marriages and conduct funeral liturgies.[[324]](#cite_note-335) Only bishops and priests can administer the sacraments of the Eucharist, reconciliation (penance) and anointing of the sick.[[325]](#cite_note-336)[[326]](#cite_note-337) Only bishops can administer the sacrament of Holy Orders, which [ordains](/wiki/Ordination "Ordination") someone into the clergy.[[327]](#cite_note-OneFaith114-338)

#### Matrimony

Main article: [Marriage in the Catholic Church](/wiki/Marriage_in_the_Catholic_Church "Marriage in the Catholic Church")

See also: [Catholic teachings on sexual morality](/wiki/Catholic_teachings_on_sexual_morality "Catholic teachings on sexual morality")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Jf9694Wedding_San_Nicolas_Church_Tolentine_Marriage_Pampangafvf_02.JPG/250px-Jf9694Wedding_San_Nicolas_Church_Tolentine_Marriage_Pampangafvf_02.JPG)](/wiki/File:Jf9694Wedding_San_Nicolas_Church_Tolentine_Marriage_Pampangafvf_02.JPG)

Wedding mass in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines")

The Catholic Church teaches that marriage is a social and spiritual bond between a man and a woman, ordered towards the good of the spouses and procreation of children; according to Catholic teachings on sexual morality, it is the only appropriate context for sexual activity. A Catholic marriage, or any marriage between baptized individuals of any Christian denomination, is viewed as a sacrament. A sacramental marriage, once consummated, cannot be dissolved except by death.[[328]](#cite_note-339)[[note 12]](#cite_note-342) The church recognizes certain [conditions](/wiki/Marriage_(Catholic_Church)#Conditions_for_a_valid_marriage_of_Catholics "Marriage (Catholic Church)"), such as freedom of consent, as required for any marriage to be valid; In addition, the church sets specific rules and norms, known as [canonical form](/wiki/Marriage_(Catholic_Church)#Canonical_form "Marriage (Catholic Church)"), that Catholics must follow.[[331]](#cite_note-343)

The church does not recognize divorce as ending a valid marriage and allows state-recognized divorce only as a means of protecting the property and well-being of the spouses and any children. However, consideration of particular cases by the competent ecclesiastical tribunal can lead to declaration of the invalidity of a marriage, a declaration usually referred to as an [annulment](/wiki/Annulment_(Catholic_Church) "Annulment (Catholic Church)"). Remarriage following a divorce is not permitted unless the prior marriage was declared invalid.[[332]](#cite_note-344)

## Liturgy

Main articles: [Catholic liturgy](/wiki/Catholic_liturgy "Catholic liturgy") and [Eastern Catholic liturgy](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_liturgy "Eastern Catholic liturgy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Thebible33.jpg/250px-Thebible33.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thebible33.jpg)

Catholic religious objects – [Holy Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible"), [crucifix](/wiki/Crucifix "Crucifix") and [rosary](/wiki/Rosary "Rosary")

Among the 24 autonomous (*[sui iuris](/wiki/Sui_iuris "Sui iuris")*) churches, numerous liturgical and other traditions exist, called rites, which reflect historical and cultural diversity rather than differences in belief.[[333]](#cite_note-345) In the definition of the [Code of Canons of the Eastern Churches](/wiki/Code_of_Canons_of_the_Eastern_Churches "Code of Canons of the Eastern Churches"), "a rite is the liturgical, theological, spiritual, and disciplinary patrimony, culture and circumstances of history of a distinct people, by which its own manner of living the faith is manifested in each Church *sui iuris*".[[334]](#cite_note-CCEO28-346)

The liturgy of the sacrament of the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist"), called the [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") in the West and [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy") or other names in the East, is the principal liturgy of the Catholic Church.[[335]](#cite_note-347) This is because it is considered the propitiatory sacrifice of Christ himself.[[336]](#cite_note-348) Eastern Catholic Churches have their [own rites](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_liturgy "Eastern Catholic liturgy"): the liturgies of the Eucharist and the other sacraments vary from rite to rite, reflecting different theological emphases.
