The [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite") is the most common [rite of worship](/wiki/Catholic_liturgical_rites "Catholic liturgical rites") used by the Catholic Church, with the [Ordinary Form of the Roman Rite](/wiki/Ordinary_Form_of_the_Roman_Rite "Ordinary Form of the Roman Rite") form of the Mass. Its use is found worldwide, originating in Rome and spreading throughout Europe, influencing and eventually supplanting local rites.[[337]](#cite_note-349) The present ordinary form of Mass in the Roman Rite, found in the post-1969 editions of the [Roman Missal](/wiki/Roman_Missal "Roman Missal"), is usually celebrated in the local [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") language, using an officially approved translation from the original text in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin").[[338]](#cite_note-350)

Its most widely used [form](/wiki/Mass_of_Paul_VI "Mass of Paul VI") was [promulgated](/wiki/Missale_Romanum_(apostolic_constitution) "Missale Romanum (apostolic constitution)") by [Pope Paul VI](/wiki/Pope_Paul_VI "Pope Paul VI") in 1969 and [revised](/wiki/Liturgiam_Authenticam "Liturgiam Authenticam") by [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") in 2001. In certain circumstances, the [1962 form](/wiki/1962_Roman_Missal "1962 Roman Missal") of the Roman Rite remains authorized in the Latin Church.

Since 2014, clergy in the small [personal ordinariates](/wiki/Personal_ordinariate "Personal ordinariate") set up for groups of former Anglicans under the terms of the 2009 document *[Anglicanorum Coetibus](/wiki/Anglicanorum_Coetibus "Anglicanorum Coetibus")*[[339]](#cite_note-Anglicanorum_Coetibus-351) are permitted to use a variation of the Roman Rite called "Divine Worship" or, less formally, "Ordinariate Use",[[340]](#cite_note-352) which incorporates elements of the [Anglican liturgy](/wiki/Christian_liturgy#Anglican_Communion "Christian liturgy") and traditions.[[note 13]](#cite_note-353)

In the [Archdiocese of Milan](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Milan "Archdiocese of Milan"), with around five million Catholics the largest in Europe,[[341]](#cite_note-354) Mass is celebrated according to the [Ambrosian Rite](/wiki/Ambrosian_Rite "Ambrosian Rite"). Other [Latin Church rites](/wiki/Latin_liturgical_rites "Latin liturgical rites") include the [Mozarabic](/wiki/Mozarabic_Rite "Mozarabic Rite")[[342]](#cite_note-355) and those of some religious institutes.[[343]](#cite_note-356) These liturgical rites have an antiquity of at least 200 years before 1570, the date of Pope Pius V's *[Quo primum](/wiki/Quo_primum "Quo primum")*, and were thus allowed to continue.[[344]](#cite_note-357)

### Eastern rites

Main article: [Catholic particular churches and liturgical rites § Eastern rites](/wiki/Catholic_particular_churches_and_liturgical_rites#Eastern_rites "Catholic particular churches and liturgical rites")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Crowning_in_Syro-Malabar_Nasrani_Wedding_by_Mar_Gregory_Karotemprel.jpg/250px-Crowning_in_Syro-Malabar_Nasrani_Wedding_by_Mar_Gregory_Karotemprel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crowning_in_Syro-Malabar_Nasrani_Wedding_by_Mar_Gregory_Karotemprel.jpg)

[East Syrian Rite](/wiki/East_Syrian_Rite "East Syrian Rite") [wedding crowning](/wiki/Wedding_crowning "Wedding crowning") celebrated by a bishop of the [Syro-Malabar Catholic Church](/wiki/Syro-Malabar_Catholic_Church "Syro-Malabar Catholic Church") in India, one of the 23 Eastern Catholic Churches in [full communion](/wiki/Full_communion "Full communion") with the pope and the Catholic Church

The Eastern Catholic Churches share common patrimony and liturgical rites as their counterparts, including [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") and other [Eastern Christian](/wiki/Eastern_Christian "Eastern Christian") churches who are no longer in communion with the Holy See. These include churches that historically developed in Russia, Caucasus, the Balkans, North Eastern Africa, India and the Middle East. The Eastern Catholic Churches are groups of faithful who have either never been out of communion with the Holy See or who have restored communion with it at the cost of breaking communion with their associates of the same tradition.[[345]](#cite_note-358)

The [liturgical rites of the Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_liturgy "Eastern Catholic liturgy") include the [Byzantine Rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite") (in its Antiochian, Greek and Slavonic [recensions](/wiki/Use_(liturgy) "Use (liturgy)")), the [Alexandrian Rite](/wiki/Alexandrian_Rite "Alexandrian Rite"), the [West Syrian Rite](/wiki/West_Syrian_Rite "West Syrian Rite"), the [Armenian Rite](/wiki/Armenian_Rite "Armenian Rite"), and the [East Syriac Rite](/wiki/East_Syriac_Rite "East Syriac Rite"). Eastern Catholic Churches have the autonomy to set the particulars of their liturgical forms and worship, within certain limits to protect the "accurate observance" of their liturgical tradition.[[346]](#cite_note-359)

In the past, some of the rites used by the Eastern Catholic Churches were subject to a degree of [liturgical Latinization](/wiki/Liturgical_Latinization "Liturgical Latinization"). In recent years Eastern Catholic Churches have returned to traditional Eastern practices in accord with the 1964 [Vatican II](/wiki/Vatican_II "Vatican II") decree *[Orientalium Ecclesiarum](/wiki/Orientalium_Ecclesiarum "Orientalium Ecclesiarum")*.[[347]](#cite_note-360) Each church has its own [liturgical calendar](/wiki/Liturgical_calendar "Liturgical calendar").[[348]](#cite_note-361)

## Social, moral and cultural issues

### Catholic social teaching

Main article: [Catholic social teaching](/wiki/Catholic_social_teaching "Catholic social teaching")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Pope_Francis_Korea_Haemi_Castle_19_%284x5_cropped%29.jpg/250px-Pope_Francis_Korea_Haemi_Castle_19_%284x5_cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pope_Francis_Korea_Haemi_Castle_19_(4x5_cropped).jpg)

On 24 May 2015, [Pope Francis](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis") issued the *[Laudato si'](/wiki/Laudato_si%27 "Laudato si'")*, an [encyclical](/wiki/Encyclical "Encyclical") that deals with questions such as consumerism, responsible development and environmental degradation.

Catholic social teaching, reflecting the concern Jesus showed for the impoverished, places a heavy emphasis on the [corporal works of mercy](/wiki/Corporal_works_of_mercy "Corporal works of mercy") and the [spiritual works of mercy](/wiki/Spiritual_works_of_mercy "Spiritual works of mercy"), namely the support and concern for the sick, the poor and the afflicted.[[349]](#cite_note-362)[[350]](#cite_note-Compendium_of_the_CCC,_388-363) Church teaching calls for a preferential [option for the poor](/wiki/Option_for_the_poor "Option for the poor") while canon law prescribes that "The Christian faithful are also obliged to promote [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice") and, mindful of the precept of the Lord, to assist the poor."[[351]](#cite_note-Cann22-364) Its foundations are widely considered to have been laid by Pope Leo XIII's 1891 encyclical letter *[Rerum novarum](/wiki/Rerum_novarum "Rerum novarum")* which upholds the rights and dignity of labour and the right of workers to form unions.[[352]](#cite_note-365)[[353]](#cite_note-366)

### Social services

Main articles: [Catholic Church and health care](/wiki/Catholic_Church_and_health_care "Catholic Church and health care") and [Catholic education](/wiki/Catholic_school "Catholic school")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/MotherTeresa_090.jpg/250px-MotherTeresa_090.jpg)](/wiki/File:MotherTeresa_090.jpg)

[Saint Teresa of Calcutta](/wiki/Mother_Teresa "Mother Teresa") advocated for the sick, the poor and the needy by practising the acts of [corporal works of mercy](/wiki/Corporal_works_of_mercy "Corporal works of mercy"). She was awarded the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") in 1979.

The Catholic Church is the largest non-government provider of education and medical services in the world.[[206]](#cite_note-Geopolitics-215) In 2010 the Catholic Church's Pontifical Council for Pastoral Assistance to Health Care Workers said that the church manages 26% of health care facilities in the world, including hospitals, clinics, orphanages, pharmacies and centres for those with leprosy.[[354]](#cite_note-367)

The church has always been involved in education, since the founding of the [first universities](/wiki/Medieval_university "Medieval university") of Europe.[[70]](#cite_note-auto-75) It runs and sponsors thousands of primary and secondary schools, [colleges and universities](/wiki/Catholic_higher_education "Catholic higher education") throughout the world[[355]](#cite_note-368)[[7]](#cite_note-Vermont_winter-7) and operates the world's largest non-governmental school system.[[356]](#cite_note-369)

Religious institutes for women have played a particularly prominent role in the provision of health and education services,[[357]](#cite_note-nunsworldwide-370) as with orders such as the [Sisters of Mercy](/wiki/Sisters_of_Mercy "Sisters of Mercy"), [Little Sisters of the Poor](/wiki/Little_Sisters_of_the_Poor "Little Sisters of the Poor"), the Missionaries of Charity, the [Sisters of St. Joseph of the Sacred Heart](/wiki/Sisters_of_St._Joseph_of_the_Sacred_Heart "Sisters of St. Joseph of the Sacred Heart"), the [Sisters of the Blessed Sacrament](/wiki/Sisters_of_the_Blessed_Sacrament "Sisters of the Blessed Sacrament") and the [Daughters of Charity of Saint Vincent de Paul](/wiki/Daughters_of_Charity_of_Saint_Vincent_de_Paul "Daughters of Charity of Saint Vincent de Paul").[[358]](#cite_note-371) The Catholic nun [Mother Teresa](/wiki/Mother_Teresa "Mother Teresa") of [Calcutta, India](/wiki/Calcutta,_India "Calcutta, India"), founder of the Missionaries of Charity, was awarded the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") in 1979 for her humanitarian work among India's poor.[[359]](#cite_note-372) Bishop [Carlos Filipe Ximenes Belo](/wiki/Carlos_Filipe_Ximenes_Belo "Carlos Filipe Ximenes Belo") won the same award in 1996 for "work towards a just and peaceful solution to the conflict in [East Timor](/wiki/East_Timor "East Timor")".[[360]](#cite_note-373)

The church is also actively engaged in international aid and development through organizations such as [Catholic Relief Services](/wiki/Catholic_Relief_Services "Catholic Relief Services"), [Caritas Internationalis](/wiki/Caritas_Internationalis "Caritas Internationalis"), [Aid to the Church in Need](/wiki/Aid_to_the_Church_in_Need "Aid to the Church in Need"), refugee advocacy groups such as the [Jesuit Refugee Service](/wiki/Jesuit_Refugee_Service "Jesuit Refugee Service") and community aid groups such as the [Saint Vincent de Paul Society](/wiki/Saint_Vincent_de_Paul_Society "Saint Vincent de Paul Society").[[361]](#cite_note-374)

### Sexual morality

Main articles: [Catholic theology of sexuality](/wiki/Catholic_theology_of_sexuality "Catholic theology of sexuality"), [Catholic theology of the body](/wiki/Catholic_theology_of_the_body "Catholic theology of the body"), and [Marriage in the Catholic Church](/wiki/Marriage_in_the_Catholic_Church "Marriage in the Catholic Church")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Hans_Memling_-_Allegory_with_a_Virgin_-_WGA14896.jpg/250px-Hans_Memling_-_Allegory_with_a_Virgin_-_WGA14896.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hans_Memling_-_Allegory_with_a_Virgin_-_WGA14896.jpg)

An [allegory](/wiki/Allegory "Allegory") of chastity by [Hans Memling](/wiki/Hans_Memling "Hans Memling")

#### Chastity and marriage

In the church's teaching, sexual activity should be reserved to married couples without artificial birth control. Marriage is considered the only appropriate context for sexual activity,[[362]](#cite_note-2337_-375) whether in a [sacramental marriage](/wiki/Marriage_in_the_Catholic_Church "Marriage in the Catholic Church") among Christians or in a [natural marriage](/wiki/Natural_marriage "Natural marriage") where one or both spouses are unbaptized. Even in romantic relationships, including [engagement to marriage](/wiki/Engagement_to_marriage "Engagement to marriage"), partners are called to abstain from sexual activity, in order to test mutual respect and fidelity.[[363]](#cite_note-376)

Chastity in marriage requires, in particular, conjugal fidelity and protecting the fecundity of marriage. The couple must foster trust and honesty as well as spiritual and physical intimacy. Sexual activity must always be open to the transmission of new life;[[364]](#cite_note-377) the church calls this the procreative significance. It must likewise always bring a couple together in love; the church calls this the unitive significance.[[365]](#cite_note-humanaevitae-378) Artificial [contraception](/wiki/Contraception "Contraception") and certain other [sexual practices](/wiki/Catholic_teachings_on_sexual_morality#Teachings_on_specific_subjects "Catholic teachings on sexual morality") are not permitted, although [natural family planning](/wiki/Natural_family_planning "Natural family planning") methods are permitted to provide healthy spacing between births, or to postpone children for a just reason.[[366]](#cite_note-379)

Church teachings about sexuality have become an issue of increasing controversy in the Western world, especially after the close of the Second Vatican Council in the 1960s, due to changing cultural attitudes described as the [sexual revolution](/wiki/Sexual_revolution "Sexual revolution").[[365]](#cite_note-humanaevitae-378) Pope Francis said in 2015 that he was worried that the church has grown "obsessed" with issues such as abortion, [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage") and [contraception](/wiki/Contraception "Contraception"), and for prioritizing moral doctrines over helping the poor and marginalized.[[367]](#cite_note-380)[[368]](#cite_note-381)

#### Homosexuality

Main article: [Homosexuality and the Catholic Church](/wiki/Homosexuality_and_the_Catholic_Church "Homosexuality and the Catholic Church")

The Catholic Church teaches that "homosexual acts" are "contrary to the natural law", "acts of grave depravity" and "under no circumstances can they be approved", but that persons experiencing homosexual tendencies must be accorded respect and dignity.[[369]](#cite_note-ccc2357-2359-382) According to the *[Catechism of the Catholic Church](/wiki/Catechism_of_the_Catholic_Church "Catechism of the Catholic Church")*,

> The number of men and women who have deep-seated homosexual tendencies is not negligible. This inclination, which is objectively disordered, constitutes for most of them a trial. They must be accepted with respect, compassion, and sensitivity. Every sign of unjust discrimination in their regard should be avoided...
> Homosexual persons are called to chastity. By the virtues of self-mastery that teach them inner freedom, at times by the support of disinterested friendship, by prayer and sacramental grace, they can and should gradually and resolutely approach Christian perfection.[[369]](#cite_note-ccc2357-2359-382)[[note 14]](#cite_note-387)

Orthodox Catholic groups, such as Building Catholic Futures, encourage parishes to incorporate celibate gay people into the church communities.[[374]](#cite_note-388)
Certain [dissenting](/wiki/Dissent "Dissent") Catholic groups, such as [DignityUSA](/wiki/DignityUSA "DignityUSA"), [oppose the position of the Catholic Church](/wiki/Dissent_from_Catholic_teaching_on_homosexuality "Dissent from Catholic teaching on homosexuality") and seek to change it and to educate Catholics on LGBT issues.[[375]](#cite_note-389) the Catholic Church has banned all such groups from church property.[[376]](#cite_note-390)

#### Divorce and declarations of nullity

Main article: [Declaration of nullity](/wiki/Declaration_of_nullity "Declaration of nullity")

Further information: [Divorce law by country](/wiki/Divorce_law_by_country "Divorce law by country")

Canon law makes no provision for divorce between baptized individuals, as a valid, consummated sacramental marriage is considered to be a lifelong bond.[[377]](#cite_note-391) However, a declaration of nullity may be granted when the proof is produced that essential conditions for contracting a valid marriage were absent from the beginning—in other words, that the marriage was not valid due to some impediment. A declaration of nullity, commonly called an annulment, is a judgement on the part of an [ecclesiastical tribunal](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_tribunal "Ecclesiastical tribunal") determining that a marriage was invalidly attempted.[[378]](#cite_note-392)

Marriages among unbaptized individuals may be dissolved with papal permission under certain situations, such as a desire to marry a Catholic, under [Pauline](/wiki/Pauline_privilege "Pauline privilege") or [Petrine privilege](/wiki/Petrine_privilege "Petrine privilege").[[329]](#cite_note-Gantley-340)[[330]](#cite_note-Doors-341) An attempt at remarriage following divorce without a declaration of nullity places "the remarried spouse ... in a situation of public and permanent adultery". An innocent spouse who lives in continence following divorce, or couples who live in continence following a civil divorce for a grave cause, do not sin.[[379]](#cite_note-393)

Worldwide, diocesan tribunals completed over 49000 cases for nullity of marriage in 2006. Over the past 30 years about 55 to 70% of annulments have occurred in the United States. The growth in annulments has been substantial; in the United States, 27,000 marriages were annulled in 2006, compared to 338 in 1968. However, approximately 200,000 married Catholics in the United States divorce each year; 10 million total as of 2006[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Catholic_Church&action=edit).[[380]](#cite_note-Soule-394)[[note 15]](#cite_note-396) Divorce is increasing in some predominantly Catholic countries in Europe.[[382]](#cite_note-397)

#### Contraception and abortion

Main article: [Christian views on birth control § Catholicism](/wiki/Christian_views_on_birth_control#Catholicism "Christian views on birth control")

See also: [Catholic Church and HIV/AIDS](/wiki/Catholic_Church_and_HIV/AIDS "Catholic Church and HIV/AIDS") and [Religious response to assisted reproductive technology § Catholicism](/wiki/Religious_response_to_assisted_reproductive_technology#Catholicism "Religious response to assisted reproductive technology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Paulaudenece1977.jpg/250px-Paulaudenece1977.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paulaudenece1977.jpg)

[Pope Paul VI](/wiki/Pope_Paul_VI "Pope Paul VI") issued *[Humanae vitae](/wiki/Humanae_vitae "Humanae vitae")* on 25 July 1968.

The church teaches that [sexual intercourse](/wiki/Human_reproduction#Copulation "Human reproduction") should only take place between a man and woman who are married to each other, and should be without the use of [birth control](/wiki/Birth_control "Birth control") or [contraception](/wiki/Contraception "Contraception"). In his encyclical *[Humanae vitae](/wiki/Humanae_vitae "Humanae vitae")*[[383]](#cite_note-humanae-398) (1968) [Pope Paul VI](/wiki/Pope_Paul_VI "Pope Paul VI") firmly rejected all artificial contraception, thus contradicting dissenters in the church who saw the [birth control pill](/wiki/Birth_control_pill "Birth control pill") as an ethically justifiable method of [contraception](/wiki/Contraception "Contraception"), though he permitted the regulation of births by means of natural family planning (NFP.) This teaching was continued especially by John Paul II in his encyclical **[Evangelium Vitae](/wiki/Evangelium_Vitae "Evangelium Vitae")**, where he clarified the church's position on artificial contraception, [abortion](/wiki/Catholic_Church_and_abortion "Catholic Church and abortion") and [euthanasia](/wiki/Euthanasia "Euthanasia") by condemning them as part of a "culture of death" and calling instead for a "[culture of life](/wiki/Culture_of_life "Culture of life")".[[384]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBokenkotter200427,_154,_493–94-399)

Many Western Catholics have voiced significant disagreement with the church's teaching on contraception.[[385]](#cite_note-400) Overturning the church's teaching on this point features high on progressive agendas.[[386]](#cite_note-401) [Catholics for Choice](/wiki/Catholics_for_Choice "Catholics for Choice"), a political lobbyist group that is not associated with the Catholic Church, stated in 1998 that 96% of American Catholic women had used contraceptives at some point in their lives and that 72% of Catholics believed that one could be a good Catholic without obeying the church's teaching on birth control.[[387]](#cite_note-cath_choice-402) Use of natural family planning methods among United States Catholics purportedly is low, although the number cannot be known with certainty.[[note 16]](#cite_note-405) As Catholic health providers are among the largest providers of services to patients with [HIV/AIDS](/wiki/HIV/AIDS "HIV/AIDS") worldwide, there is significant controversy within and outside the church regarding the use of condoms as a means of limiting new infections, as [condom](/wiki/Condom "Condom") use ordinarily constitutes prohibited contraceptive use.[[390]](#cite_note-CNS.AIDS-406)

Similarly, the Catholic Church opposes [artificial insemination](/wiki/Artificial_insemination "Artificial insemination") regardless of whether it is homologous (from the husband) or heterologous (from a [donor](/wiki/Sperm_donation "Sperm donation")) and [in vitro fertilization](/wiki/In_vitro_fertilization "In vitro fertilization") (IVF), saying that the artificial process replaces the love and conjugal act between a husband and wife.[[391]](#cite_note-407) In addition, it opposes IVF because it might cause disposal of embryos; Catholics believe an embryo is an individual with a [soul](/wiki/Soul "Soul") who must be treated as such.[[392]](#cite_note-408) For this reason, the church also opposes abortion.[[393]](#cite_note-409)

The Catholic Church oppose all forms of [abortion](/wiki/Abortion "Abortion") procedures whose direct purpose is to destroy a [zygote](/wiki/Zygote "Zygote"), [blastocyst](/wiki/Blastocyst "Blastocyst"), [embryo](/wiki/Embryo "Embryo") or [fetus](/wiki/Fetus "Fetus"), since it holds that "human life must be respected and protected absolutely from the moment of [conception](/wiki/Fertilisation "Fertilisation"). From the first moment of his existence, a human being must be recognized as having the rights of a person – among which is the inviolable [right of every innocent being to life](/wiki/Right_to_life "Right to life")".[[394]](#cite_note-Catechism-410) However, the church does recognize as morally legitimate certain acts which [indirectly](/wiki/Indirect_abortion "Indirect abortion") result in the death of the [fetus](/wiki/Fetus "Fetus"). The [1983 *Code of Canon Law*](/wiki/1983_Code_of_Canon_Law "1983 Code of Canon Law") imposes [automatic (*latae sententiae*)](/wiki/Latae_sententiae "Latae sententiae") [excommunication](/wiki/Excommunication_(Catholic_Church) "Excommunication (Catholic Church)") on [Latin Catholics](/wiki/Latin_Catholics "Latin Catholics") who actually procure an abortion,[[395]](#cite_note-411) if they fulfill the conditions for being subject to such a sanction.[[396]](#cite_note-412)

Due to the anti-abortion stance, some Catholics oppose receiving vaccines derived from fetal cells obtained via abortion. On 21 December 2020, and regarding [COVID-19 vaccination](/wiki/COVID-19_vaccination "COVID-19 vaccination"), the [Congregation for the Doctrine of the Faith](/wiki/Congregation_for_the_Doctrine_of_the_Faith "Congregation for the Doctrine of the Faith") emitted a document stating that "it is morally acceptable to receive Covid-19 vaccines that have used cell lines from aborted fetuses in their research and production process" when no alternative vaccine is available, since "the moral duty to avoid such passive material cooperation is not obligatory if there is a grave danger, such as the otherwise uncontainable spread of a serious pathological agent."[[397]](#cite_note-413)[[398]](#cite_note-:0-414) The document states that receiving the vaccine does not constitute endorsement of the practice of abortion, and that "the morality of vaccination depends not only on the duty to protect one's own health, but also on the duty to pursue the common good."[[398]](#cite_note-:0-414) The document cautions further:

> Those who, however, for reasons of conscience, refuse vaccines produced with cell lines from aborted fetuses, must do their utmost to avoid, by other prophylactic means and appropriate behavior, becoming vehicles for the transmission of the infectious agent. In particular, they must avoid any risk to the health of those who cannot be vaccinated for medical or other reasons, and who are the most vulnerable.[[398]](#cite_note-:0-414)

### Death penalty and euthanasia

Main article: [Catholic Church and capital punishment](/wiki/Catholic_Church_and_capital_punishment "Catholic Church and capital punishment")

The Catholic Church is committed to the worldwide abolition of the death penalty in any circumstance.[[399]](#cite_note-:16-415) The current *[Catechism of the Catholic Church](/wiki/Catechism_of_the_Catholic_Church "Catechism of the Catholic Church")* teaches that "in the light of the [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel")" the death penalty is "inadmissible because it is an attack on the inviolability and [dignity of the person](/wiki/Human_dignity "Human dignity")" and that the Catholic Church "works with determination for its abolition worldwide."[[400]](#cite_note-:02-416) In his 2020 encyclical *[Fratelli tutti](/wiki/Fratelli_tutti "Fratelli tutti")*, Francis repeated that the death penalty is "inadmissible" and that "there can be no stepping back from this position".[[401]](#cite_note-:112-417) On 9 January 2022 Pope Francis stated in his annual speech to Vatican ambassadors: "The death penalty cannot be employed for a purported state justice, since it does not constitute a deterrent nor render justice to victims, but only fuels the thirst for vengeance".[[402]](#cite_note-418)

There is controversy about whether the Catholic Church considers the death penalty intrinsically evil.[[403]](#cite_note-:1-419) The American Archbishop [José Horacio Gómez](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Horacio_G%C3%B3mez "José Horacio Gómez")[[403]](#cite_note-:1-419) and the Catholic philosopher [Edward Feser](/wiki/Edward_Feser "Edward Feser") argue that this is a matter of prudential judgement and that the church does not teach this as a *de fide* statement;[[404]](#cite_note-420) others, such as Cardinals [Charles Maung Bo](/wiki/Charles_Maung_Bo "Charles Maung Bo") and [Rino Fisichella](/wiki/Rino_Fisichella "Rino Fisichella"), state that it does.[[403]](#cite_note-:1-419)

The Catholic Church opposes active euthanasia and [physician-assisted suicide](/wiki/Physician-assisted_suicide "Physician-assisted suicide") on the grounds that life is a gift from God and should not be prematurely shortened. However, the church allows dying people to refuse [extraordinary treatments](/wiki/Dysthanasia "Dysthanasia") that would minimally prolong life without hope of recovery.[[405]](#cite_note-Dowbiggin98-421)[[406]](#cite_note-422)

### Holy orders and women

Main articles: [Ordination of women in the Catholic Church](/wiki/Ordination_of_women_in_the_Catholic_Church "Ordination of women in the Catholic Church") and [Women in the Catholic Church](/wiki/Women_in_the_Catholic_Church "Women in the Catholic Church")

Women and men religious engage in a variety of occupations such as contemplative prayer, teaching, providing health care, and working as missionaries.[[357]](#cite_note-nunsworldwide-370)[[407]](#cite_note-news.bbc.co.uk-423) [Catholic women](/wiki/Catholic_Church_and_women "Catholic Church and women") have played diverse roles in the life of the church, with religious institutes providing a formal space for their participation and [convents](/wiki/Convent "Convent") providing spaces for their self-government, prayer and influence through many centuries. [Religious sisters](/wiki/Religious_sisters "Religious sisters") and [nuns](/wiki/Nun "Nun") have been extensively involved in developing and running the church's worldwide health and education service networks.[[408]](#cite_note-424)

[Holy Orders](/wiki/Holy_Orders "Holy Orders") are reserved for men. Efforts in support of the [ordination of women](/wiki/Catholic_Church_doctrine_on_the_ordination_of_women "Catholic Church doctrine on the ordination of women") to the priesthood led to several rulings by the Roman Curia or popes against the proposal, as in *[Declaration on the Question of the Admission of Women to the Ministerial Priesthood](/wiki/Declaration_on_the_Question_of_the_Admission_of_Women_to_the_Ministerial_Priesthood "Declaration on the Question of the Admission of Women to the Ministerial Priesthood")* (1976), *[Mulieris Dignitatem](/wiki/Mulieris_Dignitatem "Mulieris Dignitatem")* (1988) and *[Ordinatio sacerdotalis](/wiki/Ordinatio_sacerdotalis "Ordinatio sacerdotalis")* (1994). According to the latest ruling, found in *Ordinatio sacerdotalis*, [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") affirmed that the Catholic Church "does not consider herself authorised to admit women to priestly ordination".[[409]](#cite_note-425)

In defiance of these rulings, independent opposition groups such as [Roman Catholic Womenpriests](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Womenpriests "Roman Catholic Womenpriests") have performed ceremonies they affirm as sacramental ordinations, with, reputedly, an ordaining male Catholic bishop in the first few instances, which, according to [canon law](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law"), are both illicit and invalid and considered mere *simulations*[[410]](#cite_note-cann1379-426) of the sacrament of ordination.[[411]](#cite_note-RCWP-427)[[note 17]](#cite_note-428) The [Congregation for the Doctrine of the Faith](/wiki/Congregation_for_the_Doctrine_of_the_Faith "Congregation for the Doctrine of the Faith") responded by issuing a statement clarifying that any Catholic bishops involved in ordination ceremonies for women, as well as the women themselves if they were Catholic, would automatically receive the penalty of [excommunication](/wiki/Excommunication "Excommunication") (*[latae sententiae](/wiki/Latae_sententiae "Latae sententiae")*, literally "with the sentence already applied", i.e. automatically), citing canon 1378 of [canon law](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law") and other church laws.[[412]](#cite_note-429)

### Sexual abuse cases

Main article: [Catholic Church sexual abuse cases](/wiki/Catholic_Church_sexual_abuse_cases "Catholic Church sexual abuse cases")

From the 1990s the issue of [sexual abuse of minors](/wiki/Sexual_abuse_of_minors "Sexual abuse of minors") by Catholic clergy and other church members has become the subject of civil litigation, criminal prosecution, media coverage and public debate in [countries around the world](/wiki/Catholic_Church_sex_abuse_cases_by_country "Catholic Church sex abuse cases by country"). Many Catholic bishops and other officials had protected priests accused of sexual abuse, and transferred them to other assignments elsewhere, where they continued to commit sex crimes against children. There has been resultant extensive public criticism of the church.[[413]](#cite_note-430)

In response to the scandals, formal procedures have been established to help prevent abuse, encourage the reporting of any abuse that occurs and to handle such reports promptly, although groups representing victims have disputed their effectiveness.[[414]](#cite_note-431) In 2014, Pope Francis instituted the [Pontifical Commission for the Protection of Minors](/wiki/Pontifical_Commission_for_the_Protection_of_Minors "Pontifical Commission for the Protection of Minors") for the safeguarding of minors.[[415]](#cite_note-hspo.b0199/00444.2014.03.22-432)

### Environmental

The church has also addressed stewardship of the natural environment, and its relationship to other social and theological teachings. In the document *[Laudato si'](/wiki/Laudato_si%27 "Laudato si'")*, dated 24 May 2015, Pope Francis critiques [consumerism](/wiki/Consumerism "Consumerism") and [irresponsible development](/wiki/Overdevelopment "Overdevelopment"), and laments [environmental degradation](/wiki/Environmental_degradation "Environmental degradation") and [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change").[[416]](#cite_note-NYT-433) The pope expressed concern that the warming of the planet is a symptom of a greater problem: the developed world's indifference to the destruction of the planet as humans pursue short-term economic gains.[[417]](#cite_note-434)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** This term may either refer to the entire church, including both the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") and the [Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches"), or to the Latin Church specifically.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-13)** While the Catholic Church considers itself to be the authentic continuation of the Christian community founded by Jesus Christ, it teaches that other Christian churches and communities can be in an imperfect communion with the Catholic Church.[[10]](#cite_note-note1cite1-11)[[11]](#cite_note-note1cite2-12)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-22)** Quote of St Ignatius to the Smyrnaeans (c. 110 AD)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-RCHolySeeR_31-0)** Examples uses of "Roman Catholic" by the Holy See: the encyclicals [*Divini Illius Magistri*](https://www.vatican.va/holy_father/pius_xi/encyclicals/documents/hf_p-xi_enc_31121929_divini-illius-magistri_en.html) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20100923233927/https://www.vatican.va/holy_father/pius_xi/encyclicals/documents/hf_p-xi_enc_31121929_divini-illius-magistri_en.html) 23 September 2010 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine") of [Pope Pius XI](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XI "Pope Pius XI") and [*Humani generis*](https://www.vatican.va/holy_father/pius_xii/encyclicals/documents/hf_p-xii_enc_12081950_humani-generis_en.html) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20120419021937/https://www.vatican.va/holy_father/pius_xii/encyclicals/documents/hf_p-xii_enc_12081950_humani-generis_en.html) 19 April 2012 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine") of [Pope Pius XII](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XII "Pope Pius XII"); joint declarations signed by [Pope Benedict XVI](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XVI "Pope Benedict XVI") with [Archbishop of Canterbury Rowan Williams on 23 November 2006](https://www.vatican.va/holy_father/benedict_xvi/speeches/2006/november/documents/hf_ben-xvi_spe_20061123_common-decl_en.html) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20130302070228/https://www.vatican.va/holy_father/benedict_xvi/speeches/2006/november/documents/hf_ben-xvi_spe_20061123_common-decl_en.html) 2 March 2013 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine") and [Patriarch Bartholomew I of Constantinople on 30 November 2006.](http://www.ewtn.com/library/PAPALDOC/b16bart1decl.htm) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20110430072019/http://www.ewtn.com/library/PAPALDOC/b16bart1decl.htm) 30 April 2011 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")
5. **[^](#cite_ref-RCbishop_32-0)** Example use of "Roman" Catholic by a bishop's conference: *The Baltimore Catechism*, an official catechism authorized by the Catholic bishops of the United States, states: "That is why we are called Roman Catholics; to show that we are united to the real successor of St Peter" (Question 118) and refers to the church as the "Roman Catholic Church" under Questions 114 and 131 ([Baltimore Catechism).](http://www.cin.org/users/james/ebooks/master/baltimore/bcreed09.htm) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20150923203520/http://www.cin.org/users/james/ebooks/master/baltimore/bcreed09.htm) 23 September 2015 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")
6. **[^](#cite_ref-136)** While ruling contraception to be prohibited, Pope Paul VI did, however, consider natural family planning methods to be morally permissible if used with just cause.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-165)** According to Catholic teaching, Jesus Christ is the 'invisible Head' of the Church[[154]](#cite_note-160)[[155]](#cite_note-161)[[156]](#cite_note-162) while the pope is the 'visible Head'.[[157]](#cite_note-163)[[158]](#cite_note-164)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-173)** The last resignation occurred on 28 February 2013, when [Pope Benedict XVI](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XVI "Pope Benedict XVI") retired, citing ill health in his advanced age. The next most recent resignation occurred in 1415, as part of the [Council of Constance](/wiki/Council_of_Constance "Council of Constance")'s resolution of the [Avignon Papacy](/wiki/Avignon_Papacy "Avignon Papacy").[[165]](#cite_note-duffy415-172)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-211)** In 1992, the Vatican clarified the 1983 Code of Canon Law removed the requirement that altar servers be male; permission to use female altar servers within a diocese is at the discretion of the bishop.[[202]](#cite_note-Apostalicae86-210)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-285)** Other councils that addressed the sacraments include the [Second Council of Lyon](/wiki/Second_Council_of_Lyon "Second Council of Lyon") (1274); [Council of Florence](/wiki/Council_of_Florence "Council of Florence") (1439); as well as the [Council of Trent](/wiki/Council_of_Trent "Council of Trent") (1547)[[275]](#cite_note-1113_14-284)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-315)** For an outline of the Eucharistic liturgy in the Roman Rite, see the [side bar](#Roman_Rite_of_Mass) in the "Worship and liturgy".
12. **[^](#cite_ref-342)** Marriages involving unbaptized individuals are considered valid, but not sacramental. While sacramental marriages are insoluble, non-sacramental marriages may be dissolved under certain situations, such as a desire to marry a Catholic, under [Pauline](/wiki/Pauline_privilege "Pauline privilege") or [Petrine privilege](/wiki/Petrine_privilege "Petrine privilege").[[329]](#cite_note-Gantley-340)[[330]](#cite_note-Doors-341)
13. **[^](#cite_ref-353)** The Divine Worship variant of the Roman Rite differs from the "Anglican Use" variant, which was introduced in 1980 for the few United States parishes established in accordance with a [pastoral provision](/wiki/Pastoral_provision "Pastoral provision") for former members of the [Episcopal Church](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)") (the American branch of the Anglican Communion). Both uses adapted Anglican liturgical traditions for use within the Catholic Church.
14. **[^](#cite_ref-387)** This part of the *Catechism* was quoted by Pope Francis in a 2013 press interview in which he remarked, when asked about an individual: "I think that when you encounter a person like this [the individual he was asked about], you must make a distinction between the fact of a person being gay from the fact of being a lobby, because lobbies, all are not good. That is bad. If a person is gay and seeks the Lord and has good will, well who am I to judge them?"[[370]](#cite_note-383) This remark and others made in the same interview were seen as a change in the tone, but not in the substance of the teaching of the church,[[371]](#cite_note-384) which includes opposition to [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage").[[372]](#cite_note-1601_05-385)[[373]](#cite_note-386)
15. **[^](#cite_ref-396)** With regard to divorce in the United States, according to the Barna Group, among all who have been married, 33% have been divorced at least once; among American Catholics, 28% (the study did not track religious annulments).[[381]](#cite_note-395)
16. **[^](#cite_ref-405)** Regarding use of [natural family planning](/wiki/Natural_family_planning "Natural family planning"), in 2002, 24% of the U.S. population identified as Catholic,[[388]](#cite_note-adherents-403) but according to a 2002 study by the [Centers for Disease Control and Prevention](/wiki/Centers_for_Disease_Control_and_Prevention "Centers for Disease Control and Prevention"), of sexually active Americans avoiding pregnancy, only 1.5% were using NFP.[[389]](#cite_note-cdc-404)
17. **[^](#cite_ref-428)** According to Roman Catholic Womanpriests: "The principal consecrating Roman Catholic male bishop who ordained our first women bishops is a bishop with apostolic succession within the Roman Catholic Church in full communion with the pope."[[411]](#cite_note-RCWP-427)
